DuckDB
Home Concepts Architecture How It Works Implementation Use Cases Ecosystem FAQ Trade-offs
Intermediate

Trade-offs & Limitations

Every tool excels at something and struggles at something else. Here is an honest look at where DuckDB shines, where it falls short, and how it stacks up against the alternatives.

Performance Profile

DuckDB at a Glance

A visual snapshot of DuckDB's capability profile. Green means a core strength; yellow is adequate; red signals a known limitation.

Analytical Query Speed 95%
Vectorized columnar execution, on par with or faster than many distributed engines for single-machine data
Setup Simplicity 98%
pip install duckdb and you are running queries -- zero configuration, zero dependencies
Format Versatility 90%
Parquet, CSV, JSON, Iceberg, Lance, Excel, PostgreSQL, SQLite, and more via extensions
Write Concurrency 15%
Single-writer by design -- only one connection can write at a time
Distributed Scalability 10%
Single machine only -- no shardingSharding splits a database across multiple machines so each machine stores a subset of the data. This allows horizontal scaling beyond one server's capacity., replicationReplication copies data across multiple servers for fault tolerance and read scalability. If one server fails, others can serve the data., or cluster mode
Access Control 5%
No built-in users, roles, or row-level security -- rely on OS-level file permissions
Honest Assessment

Strengths & Limitations

✅ Strengths
  • +
    Zero-Configuration Deployment
    pip install duckdb and you are running queries. No server process, no config files, no external dependencies.
  • +
    Exceptional Analytics Performance
    Vectorized columnar execution delivers blazing-fast scans, aggregations, and joins on a single machine.
  • +
    Format Versatility
    Query Parquet, CSV, JSON, Iceberg, Lance, Excel, PostgreSQL, and SQLite -- all from the same SQL interface.
  • +
    Developer Experience
    Deep Python integration, seamless DataFrame interop with Pandas and Polars, and a friendly CLI (v1.5.0+).
  • +
    Cost
    MIT-licensedThe MIT License is one of the most permissive open-source licenses. It allows anyone to use, modify, and distribute the software -- even commercially -- with almost no restrictions. and free. Can replace expensive cloud data warehouse queries for many workloads.
  • +
    Portability
    Runs on Linux, macOS, Windows, in browsers (Wasm), on mobile devices, and in embedded systems.
⚠️ Limitations
  • –
    Single-Writer Concurrency
    Only one connection can write at a time. This is by design, not a bug -- but it rules out high-concurrency write workloads.
  • –
    Not Distributed
    Single machine only. No sharding, replication, or cluster mode. Data must fit on one node (practical limit ~2 TB).
  • –
    Not an OLTPOnline Transaction Processing. Systems optimized for fast, small, row-level reads and writes -- think e-commerce orders or banking transactions. PostgreSQL and MySQL are classic OLTP databases. Database
    Optimized for OLAPOnline Analytical Processing. Systems optimized for complex queries over large datasets -- aggregations, joins, scans. DuckDB, ClickHouse, and BigQuery are OLAP systems. (scans, aggregations, joins), not point lookups or single-row updates.
  • –
    Young Ecosystem
    Extensions are younger than PostgreSQL's ecosystem. Breaking changes can occur between versions.
  • –
    No Built-in Access Control
    No users, roles, or row-level security. You must rely on OS-level file permissions or application-layer auth.
  • –
    Storage Format Changes
    On-disk format can change between major versions, potentially requiring data re-export when upgrading.
⚠
Storage Format Warning

DuckDB's on-disk storage format can change between major versions. While backward-compatible reads are maintained within a version line, upgrading across major versions (e.g., 1.x to 2.x) may require exporting and re-importing your data. Always test upgrades on a copy first.

✔
LTSLong-Term Support. An LTS release receives bug fixes and security patches for an extended period. DuckDB introduced LTS with version 1.4, guaranteeing format stability within the LTS line. Release Strategy

Starting with version 1.4, DuckDB introduced a Long-Term Support release line. LTS versions guarantee forward-compatible storage within the same line (e.g., all 1.4.x releases can read each other's files). This significantly reduces upgrade risk for production deployments.

Alternatives

How DuckDB Compares

Click the tabs on each card to toggle between strengths, weaknesses, and ideal use cases.

DuckDB Current
Best for: single-machine analytics, local data exploration, embedded analytics
  • Zero-config, embedded deployment
  • 10-100x faster analytics than SQLite
  • Queries any file format directly
  • MIT-licensed, truly free
  • Single-writer concurrency
  • Not distributed
  • No access control
  • Format changes between versions
  • Local data analysis and exploration
  • ETL pipelines on a single machine
  • Embedded analytics in applications
  • Replacing expensive cloud queries
SQLite
Best for: OLTP, embedded transactional workloads, mobile apps
  • Battle-tested, deployed everywhere
  • Excellent for row-level inserts/updates
  • Tiny footprint, public domain
  • Rock-solid stability and compatibility
  • 10-100x slower for analytical queries
  • Row-oriented storage (bad for scans)
  • Limited SQL dialect
  • Single-writer, no concurrent writes
  • Mobile and desktop applications
  • Configuration storage
  • Small transactional workloads
  • Edge and IoT devices
ClickHouse
Best for: distributed real-time analytics at petabyte scale
  • Petabyte-scale distributed queries
  • High-concurrency read support
  • Real-time ingestion pipelines
  • Mature replication and sharding
  • Complex to set up and operate
  • Requires server infrastructure
  • Overkill for single-machine data
  • Steeper learning curve
  • Real-time analytics dashboards
  • Log and event analytics at scale
  • Time-series data warehouses
  • High-concurrency analytical APIs
PostgreSQL
Best for: general-purpose database with OLTP and some analytics
  • Full ACID, MVCC concurrency
  • Rich access control (users, roles, RLS)
  • Massive extension ecosystem
  • Strong OLTP performance
  • Much slower for analytical scans
  • Requires server setup and management
  • Row-oriented (not optimized for OLAP)
  • Heavier resource footprint
  • Web application backends
  • Mixed OLTP + light analytics
  • Enterprise with access control needs
  • Geospatial (PostGIS)
Polars
Best for: DataFrame-centric analytics in Python and Rust
  • Highly Pythonic API
  • Lazy evaluation and query optimization
  • Excellent memory efficiency
  • Fast growing community
  • Less mature SQL support
  • Fewer supported file formats
  • No persistent storage engine
  • Smaller extension ecosystem
  • Python-first data pipelines
  • Replacing Pandas for performance
  • ML feature engineering
  • Streaming DataFrame workflows
Spark
Best for: distributed batch processing across clusters
  • True distributed computing
  • Handles petabyte-scale data
  • Rich ML library (MLlib)
  • Mature ecosystem and tooling
  • Orders of magnitude more complex
  • Requires cluster infrastructure
  • High latency for small queries
  • JVM overhead and memory tuning
  • Multi-terabyte batch processing
  • Distributed ML training
  • Data lake ETL at enterprise scale
  • Streaming with Structured Streaming
BigQuery / Snowflake
Best for: managed cloud analytics with governance and scale
  • Fully managed, no ops burden
  • Elastic scaling to petabytes
  • Built-in governance and access control
  • High-concurrency multi-user support
  • Expensive at scale (per-query pricing)
  • Network latency for every query
  • Vendor lock-in risk
  • Overkill for single-user exploration
  • Enterprise data warehousing
  • Multi-team analytics platforms
  • Compliance-heavy environments
  • Workloads requiring governance
🎯 The Bottom Line

DuckDB excels when your data fits on one machine (up to ~2 TB) and your workload is analytical. It is the best choice for local data analysis, ETL pipelines, embedded analytics in applications, and replacing expensive cloud warehouse queries.

Choose something else for distributed scale, high write concurrency, or enterprise access control. The right tool depends on where your data lives, how big it is, and who needs to query it.

Local analysis ETL pipelines Embedded analytics Cloud cost reduction Data exploration Single-machine OLAP
